items.title: When order gives way to chaos—the turbulent birth of magnetic nanovortices
items.summary: Magnetic switching processes are considered a prime example of controllable physics at the nanometer scale: in certain thin-film systems, a short electrical current pulse is sufficient to reverse the magnetization in a targeted way. The underlying effect is the so-called spin–orbit torque: the curre

items.title: Companies struggle to realize circular ambitions, says researcher
items.summary: The research, conducted by Wierikx at the Department of Industrial Engineering & Innovation Sciences, is based on more than 3,000 organizational assessments. The results reveal a consistent pattern: companies are relatively strong in setting circular ambitions, but face major challenges in scaling i

items.title: Očinstvo dramatično mijenja mozak muškarca, otkriva studija
items.summary: NOVO istraživanje otkriva da i očevi prolaze kroz značajne promjene na mozgu nakon rođenja djeteta. Mozak se restrukturira kako bi se prilagodio novim zahtjevima i ojačao roditeljske vještine.

items.title: New 'AI scientists' are improving—but reveal their fundamental limits
items.summary: Many of the most exciting discoveries in science involve highly specialized knowledge and making connections between far-flung facts. Scientists must combine deep analysis with broad reasoning strategies.

items.title: Not just a fun hobby: Board games can help build connections and reduce stress
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Plymouth recently confirmed what board game fans and role-playing game (RPG) enthusiasts have known for decades: that tabletop games "enhance well-being, foster inclusion, and support learning, with strong evidence that games improve engagement."

items.title: Gold-coated optical fiber rapidly gathers microscopic targets for faster, more sensitive detection
items.summary: Osaka Metropolitan University researchers have developed a light-driven technique that quickly amasses thousands of bacteria into a single spot, boosting detection speed and sensitivity. Their approach paves the way for earlier diagnosis of disease. The study is published in Communications Physics.

items.title: Study shows how sunspot activity speeds up reentries
items.summary: It's getting crowded up there. Over the past few years, the advent of SpaceX's Starlink and other players in the mega-satellite constellation game are adding an exponential load of satellites and orbital debris to the low Earth orbit environment. But all that goes up, must eventually come

items.title: Mathematicians solve decades-old mystery about the hidden order in high-dimensional randomness
items.summary: Three mathematicians have laid out proof that solves a long-standing problem in mathematics. Even the mathematician—an Abel prize winner—that first posed the problem didn't believe it would ever be solved. The solution provides insight into high-dimensional random structures that could potentia

items.title: The impact of microplastics on ocean carbon uptake
items.summary: Marine microplastics affect algae's ability to grow and photosynthesize. Researchers have now calculated what impact this has on the greenhouse effect and the ocean's ability to absorb carbon dioxide.

items.title: Hydrous mantle minerals reveal possible source of seismic anisotropy in stagnant slabs
items.summary: Seismic waves traveling through Earth's interior often propagate at different speeds depending on their direction, a phenomenon known as seismic anisotropy. Such anisotropy is commonly detected beneath subduction zones, particularly near stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone and uppermos

items.title: Hotels strive to be found as AI models conduct travel search
items.summary: With people increasingly adopting AI to help plan their vacations, hotels are working to make sure that you check them out—and check in.

items.title: 'Patchwork families' existed more than 5,000 years ago, Neolithic DNA reveals
items.summary: Children from previous relationships growing up as siblings in a new family, couples adopting or fostering children: So-called patchwork families are a widespread way of life today. It is considered modern, but is in fact ancient, according to a recent analysis of human genetic material from the Neo

items.title: Heatstroke kills 16 in India as temperatures climb
items.summary: At least 16 people have died of heatstroke in southern India so far this summer, officials said Sunday, as a heat wave grips swathes of the country following official health warnings.

items.title: Drevni život pronađen ispod kratera asteroida u Koreji
items.summary: OTKRIĆE stromatolita ispod kratera u Južnoj Koreji sugerira da su udari asteroida mogli stvoriti hidrotermalne uvjete pogodne za razvoj ranog života, nudeći novi uvid u nastanak života na Zemlji.

items.title: Solar activity follows an 11‑year cycle. Here's how it controls eruptions and solar flares
items.summary: When you look up at the sky on a sunny day, the sun might seem like a bright spot, unchanging in the sky. But the sun is a complex, dynamic celestial body, wrapped in electrical currents and magnetic fields that constantly move and tangle as it rotates. At times the sun's surface is very active

items.title: Surprising research reveals why you shouldn't add bananas to your smoothies
items.summary: Researchers found that adding bananas to berry smoothies can dramatically reduce the body’s ability to absorb healthy flavanols. The surprising discovery shows that even simple food combinations can change how much nutrition your body actually gets.

items.title: Scientists say house cats could help unlock new cancer treatments for humans
items.summary: Scientists have cracked open the “black box” of feline cancer in a landmark study that genetically analyzed nearly 500 cat tumors from around the world. The research uncovered striking similarities between cancers in cats, dogs, and humans — including shared cancer-driving genes tied to aggressive b

items.title: Scientists discover the oldest wooden tools ever used by humans
items.summary: Scientists have uncovered the oldest known hand-held wooden tools ever used by humans — and they’re an astonishing 430,000 years old. Buried for hundreds of thousands of years at an ancient lakeside site in Greece, the carefully carved wooden objects reveal that early humans were far more skilled an

items.title: Heavily reddened quasars caught going through a 'blow-out' phase
items.summary: At the center of most large galaxies sits a supermassive black hole (SMBH). When these black holes are actively consuming material, they become incredibly luminous quasars. But some quasars appear wrapped in thick clouds of dust, making them hard to detect. In a new study, astronomers have revealed

items.title: Scientists “recharge” damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
items.summary: For millions battling chronic nerve pain, even the softest touch can feel agonizing — but scientists may have uncovered a radically new way to stop it at the source.
